title: Achieving Large Uniaxial and Homogeneous Strain in Two-Dimensional Materials
authors: Yangchen He, Jessica Kienbaum, Wuzhang Fang, Hongrui Ma, Ying Wang, Ping Yuan, Daniel A. Rhodes
abstract: Strain engineering is a powerful tool for tuning the electronic, magnetic, and topological properties of two-dimensional (2D) materials and thin films - particularly at high values of strain (>3%) where many electronic, magnetic, and structural transitions have been predicted. However, most approaches to tuning strain in 2D materials are limited below 1.5%, with poor repeatability when cycling strain and low strain transfer when cooling to cryogenic temperatures. Here, we report a high-yield sample preparation and device strain platform that overcomes these limitations, enabling precise, reversible strain tuning up to the intrinsic strain-to-failure of the materials tested herein. In addition, we show that this platform can be used to controllably design uniform linear strain gradients across of 10's of $μ$m, providing a novel route to systematically investigate flexoelectric and flexomagnetic phenomena. Using CrSBr as a model system, we demonstrate uniform uniaxial strain, up to ~4%, with negligible slippage and linear strain gradients of up to 0.06%/$μ$m. We further show that our strain approach is applicable to a broad class of 2D materials, validating its performance for three different phases of transition metal dichalcogenides: 2H-MoTe$_2$, 1T$^\prime$-MoTe$_2$ and T$_\mathrm{d}$-WTe$_2$. In T$_\mathrm{d}$-WTe$_2$, verified by theoretical calculations, we show a continuous redshift of the A$_1^3$ mode, up to a record-breaking ~5.5% strain, with a clear separation of the A$_1^3$ and A$_1^2$ modes starting at 2% strain.
summary: This paper presents a new experimental platform for applying large and uniform mechanical strain to 2D materials. By using a specialized silicon substrate functionalized with a polymer, the researchers achieved strain levels far exceeding previous limits, reaching up to 5.5% in WTe2. The method is highly reproducible, works at cryogenic temperatures, and even allows for the creation of controlled strain gradients. This enables the systematic study of strain-induced phase transitions and new physical phenomena like flexoelectricity in 2D systems.
main_problem: Existing methods for strain engineering in 2D materials are limited to low strain values (<1.5%), suffer from poor reproducibility, and exhibit low strain transfer at cryogenic temperatures.
main_result: The authors developed a high-yield, reliable platform using PCL-functionalized Si substrates and a piezo-stack to achieve large, reversible, and homogeneous uniaxial strain up to the intrinsic failure limits of materials, including a record 5.5% in Td-WTe2.
method: A piezo-stack-based strain cell is used with a custom Si substrate featuring an etched trench, functionalized with a polycaprolactone (PCL) film to enhance adhesion and strain transfer via a reversed dry transfer method.
model_or_system: The platform is demonstrated on various 2D materials, specifically CrSBr as a primary model for strain calibration, and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) including 2H-MoTe2, 1T'-MoTe2, and Td-WTe2.
key_observables: Raman spectroscopy (peak shifts, FWHM, and mode splitting), Raman mapping for strain distribution, and optical imaging of cracks/failure.
important_parameters: Strain magnitudes up to 5.5%, temperature range down to 2 K, strain gradients up to 0.06%/um, and piezoelectric voltage (Vp) as a control parameter.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the PCL layer provides sufficient adhesion and that the piezoelectric voltage serves as a linear proxy for applied strain.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the substrate setup and transfer process; Figure 5 illustrates the evolution of Raman spectra and peak positions for various MoTe2 phases under strain; supplementary figures show strain distribution maps and DFT validation.
paper_structure: Introduction to strain engineering limitations, description of the experimental strain platform and fabrication, validation using CrSBr, demonstration of broad applicability across TMD phases, and theoretical validation via DFT.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic for the strain substrate setup (a-e) and sample transfer process (g-i). (j) Enlarged view of the PCL/2D material interface, where cooling through the glass transition tem- perature leads to encapsulation, enabling high-strain with negligible slippage.

Figure 2. (a) Evolution of the Raman spectra centered near the A3 g vibrational mode of CrSBr with increasing Vp in a thin flake (d ≈20nm). (b-d) Optical images and schematics depicting the strain distribution of the CrSBr under homogeneous strain (b), partial cracking (c) and mechanical failure (d). The optical images are taken after Raman spectra collection at 0 V, 50 V, 59 V, for c-e, respectively. (b) Evolution of the A3 g mode with response to the increment of Vp towards mechanical failure (4%) in a thick sample (d ≈40nm). Raman peak indicating relaxation status (grey arrow) at high strain and broadened peak at failure, indicating disordered strain status.

Figure 3. (a) Raman spectra plotted with multiple cycles of Vp between unstrained and strained (3%) states. (b) Converted strain from (a) across the four cycles. (c) FWHM of the corresponding A3 g peak in (a). The grey dash vertical line marks the maximum Vp during the cycled test.

Figure 4. (a) Spatial map of tensile strain derived from the CrSBr A3 g Raman mode, showing homogeneous strain within the gap region (grey dashed line shows gap edge and solid lines indicate transverse film edge) and a strain gradient in the adjacent regions. (b) Strain line profiles parallel (blue) and perpendicular (red) to the strain direction, extracted from the constant strain region in (a). (d) Strain distribution extending outside the gap. By fixing the gap strain at specific levels (4%, 3%, 2%), a linear strain gradient is maintained across different strain backgrounds.

Figure 5. Evolution of Raman spectra for mechanically exfoliated (a) 2H-MoTe2, (c) 1T′-MoTe2, and (e) Td-WTe2, under increasing piezoelectric voltage (Vp). Dashed lines indicate Gaussian fits to the phonon modes exhibiting the strongest strain response. The linear evolution of the Raman peak position as a function of Vp is shown for (b) (2H-MoTe2) and (d) (1T′-MoTe2). (f) Intensity map of Td-WTe2 spectra versus Vp, highlighting the strain-induced enhancement of the A7 1 modes and the splitting of the A2 1 mode. For figure (b, d, f), the corresponding tensile strain values (right axis) were calculated using a calibration factor of 0.23% V−1, derived from the CrSBr sensor on the same setup.
